posts.title: Denzel Washington Commencement Speech – University of Pennsylvania
posts.excerpt: Denzel Washington's 2011 commencement speech at Penn is funny, moving, and unforgettable. His advice on failure, falling forward, and faith is essential viewing for every high school senior.
posts.content:
"Fall Forward" — One of the Most Quoted Commencement Speeches Ever

Denzel Washington delivered one of the most memorable commencement speeches in recent history to the University of Pennsylvania's Class of 2011. Unlike many graduation addresses heavy on platitudes, Washington's was raw, funny, deeply personal — and built around a single powerful idea: fall forward.

The Core Message: Fail Forward

Washington told graduates to embrace failure as a feature, not a bug, of a meaningful life. He used his own experiences — including being passed over for roles, facing rejection, and making mistakes — to illustrate that every setback is a setup for a comeback. His message wasn't abstract positivity. It was grounded in real experience from a career that includes two Academy Awards and decades of iconic performances.

"Fall forward. Every failed experiment is one step closer to success. You've got to take risks. And I mean that literally. In this room right now, there are future Maya Angelous and future Barack Obamas."

Why Penn?

The University of Pennsylvania — one of the eight Ivy League universities — has a long tradition of inviting cultural and intellectual figures to address its graduating class. Penn's location in Philadelphia, its world-renowned Wharton School of Business, and its emphasis on interdisciplinary education make it one of the most sought-after universities for students interested in both academic rigor and real-world impact. Explore our full guide to applying to the University of Pennsylvania.

What College Applicants Can Learn

Washington's message maps directly onto what top colleges are looking for in applicants. Admissions officers at Penn, Harvard, and other elite schools are not looking for students with perfect records who have never failed. They are looking for students who have demonstrated resilience — who have fallen down and found a way to get back up with greater clarity and purpose.

When writing your college application essays, don't hide your failures. Frame them the way Washington does: as turning points. The moment you failed a test, lost a competition, or had a plan fall apart is often the most interesting part of your story.

About the Speaker

Denzel Washington is a two-time Academy Award winner (Glory, 1990; Training Day, 2002) and one of the most acclaimed actors of his generation. He attended Fordham University in New York, where he initially majored in journalism before discovering his passion for acting. He has starred in over 50 films including Malcolm X, Philadelphia, The Equalizer, and Fences, which he also directed.

See also: Oprah's Stanford commencement address — another iconic speech from a cultural figure who built their path against significant odds.

posts.title: Mark Rober Gave the MIT Commencement Speech
posts.excerpt: Former NASA engineer and YouTube science star Mark Rober delivered MIT's 2023 commencement speech. His message about curiosity, creativity, and building things that matter is unmissable for STEM students.
posts.content:
NASA Engineer Turned YouTube Legend Addresses MIT's Class of 2023

Mark Rober — the former NASA JPL engineer who became one of YouTube's most beloved science communicators — delivered the commencement address to MIT's Class of 2023. With over 50 million YouTube subscribers and viral projects like the squirrel obstacle course and glitter bomb packages, Rober brought his trademark combination of engineering brilliance and playful creativity to one of the world's greatest technical institutions.

Why This Speech Resonates with STEM Students

Rober's speech is especially powerful because he speaks the language of MIT graduates fluently. He's not a politician or a celebrity — he's an engineer who spent nine years at NASA working on the Mars Curiosity rover before pivoting to science education on YouTube. His credibility with technical audiences is earned, not borrowed.

Key Themes

Curiosity Is a Superpower

Rober's central argument is that the ability to stay curious — to approach problems like a child experimenting without fear of being wrong — is the defining trait of the world's most innovative thinkers. This is the ethos that MIT was founded on and the quality that makes MIT graduates among the most sought-after in the world.

Build Things That Matter

Rober challenged graduates to use their technical skills on problems that actually matter — global health, climate, education, mental health. This echoes the mission of MIT's undergraduate research programs and its emphasis on technology serving humanity. Our guide to applying to MIT explores how this mission shapes what the school looks for in applicants.

The Value of Making Mistakes Publicly

One of the most striking parts of Rober's speech was his openness about failures — projects that flopped, videos that underperformed, engineering errors. He argued that being willing to fail visibly and learn from it publicly is what accelerates learning. This is directly relevant to the MIT admissions process, which explicitly looks for students comfortable with intellectual risk-taking.

What College Applicants Can Learn

For students applying to MIT, Caltech, and other STEM-focused universities, Rober's speech is a masterclass in how elite institutions think about talent. They don't want students who are good at following instructions — they want students who build things, break things, and figure out why. If your application essay is about a project you built, a problem you solved, or a question you couldn't stop thinking about, you are speaking MIT's language.

Visit MIT's official website to read more about the school's mission and values, which align closely with Rober's vision.

About the Speaker

Mark Rober graduated from Brigham Young University and earned a master's degree from the University of Southern California (USC). He worked at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory for nine years, contributing to the Mars Curiosity rover, before leaving to focus on YouTube. He founded CrunchLabs, a hands-on science subscription box for kids. His videos have collectively been viewed over 4 billion times.

posts.title: Facebook Founder Mark Zuckerberg Commencement Address | Harvard Commencement 2017
posts.excerpt: Mark Zuckerberg's 2017 Harvard commencement speech — delivered by a dropout who returned with an honorary degree — is a compelling vision of purpose, community, and the future of human connection.
posts.content:
A Dropout Returns With an Honorary Degree

In 2017, Mark Zuckerberg delivered Harvard's commencement address — twelve years after dropping out of the same school to launch Facebook from his dorm room. He received an honorary degree at the ceremony. His speech went far beyond Silicon Valley success; it was a thoughtful, sometimes personal call for a generation to find and create purpose.

The Central Idea: Purpose

Zuckerberg's central argument is that his generation faces a crisis of purpose. Not just individually — he means societally. He spoke about how previous generations found purpose through institutions: religion, unions, civic organizations. Those structures have weakened, and Facebook and other platforms have partially filled the gap. But he challenged graduates to rebuild community from the ground up.

"Purpose is that sense that we are part of something bigger than ourselves, that we are needed, that we have something better ahead to work for. Purpose is what creates true happiness."

Three Ways to Create Purpose

1. Take on Big, Meaningful Projects Together

Zuckerberg cited the moon landing, eradicating disease, and connecting the world as examples of purpose-driven goals that unite people. He challenged graduates not to settle for incremental change when transformational change is possible. This is an important mindset for college applicants — showing that you think beyond individual success signals intellectual maturity.

2. Redefine Equality

Zuckerberg spoke candidly about the privilege of his own position — attending Harvard, having a safety net to take risks. He called for creating a world where everyone has a cushion to take risks, not just those born into opportunity. For students applying to schools focused on social impact, this framing of equality is worth incorporating into your thinking.

3. Build Community

His final point was about the need to rebuild local and global community. This was prescient — the years following this speech have seen intense debate about social media's role in fragmenting rather than uniting communities.

What College Applicants Can Learn

Zuckerberg's speech is one of the most honest commencement addresses by a tech founder. He doesn't pretend Facebook is perfect or that his path should be replicated. Instead, he models the kind of self-awareness and global thinking that elite universities want to develop in their students. Read our guide to applying to Harvard as an international student to understand what this values-driven approach looks like in an admissions context.

Also see our Mark Zuckerberg alumni profile for more on his academic background and the lessons his story offers for students.

About the Speaker

Mark Zuckerberg co-founded Facebook (now Meta) in his Harvard dorm room in 2004, dropping out later that year. Meta now reaches over 3 billion monthly active users across Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp. He and his wife Priscilla Chan have pledged 99% of their Facebook shares to the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative, focused on curing disease and improving education.

posts.title: President Obama Speaks at Barnard College Commencement Ceremony
posts.excerpt: President Obama's 2012 Barnard commencement address challenged women graduates to lead boldly at a moment of historic possibility — and offered timeless advice on resilience, purpose, and the courage to be first.
posts.content:
Obama at Barnard: "Forge Your Path" in an Uncertain World

In 2012, President Barack Obama delivered the commencement address at Barnard College — the historic women's college affiliated with Columbia University in New York City. His speech came at a pivotal moment: women were outnumbering men in universities for the first time in history, yet still faced significant barriers in government, business, and the sciences. Obama challenged Barnard graduates to seize this moment of historic possibility.

The Historical Moment

Obama framed the speech around a remarkable statistic: for the first time in American history, women outnumbered men in universities, in the workforce, and in a generation that had grown up with no ceiling on their ambitions. His message wasn't simply congratulatory — it was a challenge. With greater access comes greater responsibility. "The question is not whether you'll lead," he told graduates, "but how."

Key Lessons

Resilience Over Perfection

Obama directly addressed the pressure many high-achieving women feel to be perfect — to never make mistakes, to have it all figured out. He pushed back hard against this expectation, arguing that resilience — the ability to get back up after failure — is a more valuable quality than perfection. For college applicants, this is worth internalizing. Admissions essays that show how you recovered from failure are far more compelling than essays about being perfect.

Be Willing to Be First

Some of history's most important advances came from people who were willing to be the first — the first woman in a role, the first person from their community at a certain school, the first to challenge a norm that seemed immovable. Obama challenged Barnard graduates to be willing to occupy that uncomfortable space.

Civic Engagement Is Not Optional

Obama argued that democracy requires active participation — not passive observation. He challenged graduates to engage in public life, run for office, work in public service, and not leave the arena to others. This is especially relevant for students considering careers in law, public policy, or social impact — all areas where elite university credentials open significant doors.

About Barnard and Columbia

Barnard College is one of the most prestigious women's colleges in the United States, consistently ranked among the top liberal arts colleges in the country. Its affiliation with Columbia University gives students access to all Columbia resources, cross-registration in Columbia courses, and degrees recognized by both institutions. Learn more about Columbia's admissions process in our international student guide to Columbia.

About the Speaker

Barack Obama is the 44th President of the United States and the first African American to hold the office. He graduated from Columbia University and earned his law degree from Harvard Law School. Before his presidency, he served as a U.S. Senator from Illinois and community organizer in Chicago. His commencement speeches — including addresses at Michigan, Notre Dame, and Howard — are among the most watched graduation speeches in history.

posts.title: 2024 Commencement Address by Roger Federer at Dartmouth
posts.excerpt: Roger Federer's 2024 Dartmouth commencement speech is a masterclass in embracing imperfection. One of history's greatest athletes reveals that even he only won 54% of the points he played. What does that mean for you?
posts.content:
The Greatest Tennis Player Ever Speaks at Dartmouth — And His Statistic Will Shock You

Roger Federer — winner of 20 Grand Slam titles, widely considered the greatest tennis player in history — delivered the 2024 Dartmouth College commencement address. His speech immediately went viral, and for good reason: he revealed a statistic about his own career that reframes everything you think you know about success.

The Number That Changes Everything: 54%

Federer told graduates that across his entire professional career — one of the most decorated in sports history — he won approximately 54% of the points he played. Not 80%. Not 90%. Barely more than half.

Think about that. The greatest tennis player in history lost 46 out of every 100 points he played. His point: in tennis, as in life, what separates champions from the rest is not winning every point. It is how you respond to the points you lose. It is the ability to move on, reset, and compete again without letting imperfection derail you.

"When you're playing a point, it has to be the most important thing in the world. And it is. But when it's behind you, it's behind you."

Effort Is a Choice — Every Single Time

Federer's second major theme was the distinction between outcomes (which you can't fully control) and effort (which you always can). He challenged Dartmouth graduates to focus on the one thing entirely within their power: how hard they try. This is not a cliché. Coming from someone who has played in Grand Slam finals after losing the first two sets, it carries genuine weight.

What College Applicants Can Learn

Federer's 54% statistic is the most important thing for college applicants to hear. The college admissions process is full of rejection — even the strongest applicants get turned down from their top choices. What defines you is not where you got rejected. It is how you played the next point: how you responded, what you built, and where you went from there.

Dartmouth, one of the eight Ivy League universities, actively looks for students who demonstrate this kind of resilience in their applications. Read our guide on why Ivy League admission is so competitive and what you can actually do to strengthen your candidacy.

About Dartmouth College

Dartmouth College is the smallest of the eight Ivy League universities, located in Hanover, New Hampshire. Its tight-knit community, exceptional undergraduate focus, and strong alumni network in finance, consulting, and medicine make it one of the most appealing schools for students seeking the Ivy League experience with a smaller, more personal campus. Notable Dartmouth alumni include Conan O'Brien and Dr. Seuss.

About the Speaker

Roger Federer is a Swiss professional tennis player who won 20 Grand Slam singles titles — a record at the time of his retirement in 2022. He won Wimbledon 8 times, the Australian Open 6 times, the US Open 5 times, and the French Open once. Federer is widely regarded as the most technically gifted player in the sport's history and has been ranked world No. 1 for a record 310 weeks. He retired in September 2022 after competing in the Laver Cup alongside his great rival Rafael Nadal.

posts.title: Tom Hanks Addresses the Yale Class of 2011
posts.excerpt: Tom Hanks gave one of the funniest and most unexpectedly wise commencement addresses ever at Yale in 2011 — covering typewriters, the truth, and why you should always do your homework.
posts.content:
Hollywood's Most Trusted Voice Addresses Yale's Class of 2011

Tom Hanks — two-time Academy Award winner, one of the most beloved actors in Hollywood history — delivered the 2011 Yale University commencement address. True to form, the speech was disarmingly funny, self-deprecating, and laced with genuine wisdom. Hanks spoke about truth-telling, preparation, and his unlikely path from a community college dropout to one of America's most celebrated artists.

Three Things Hanks Promised the Yale Class

Hanks structured his speech around three promises: that they would be called a fraud, that they would need to always do their homework, and that the hard times would make them who they are. Each point was delivered with humor — but each had a genuine lesson beneath it.

1. You Will Be Called a Fraud — and That's Fine

Hanks described the imposter syndrome that affects even the most successful people. He joked that throughout his career he waited for someone to "find him out" and reveal that he didn't deserve his success. His advice: ignore the voice. Show up. Do the work. The anxiety of feeling like a fraud is universal — the people who succeed are the ones who act anyway.

2. Always Do Your Homework

In one of the speech's most practical sections, Hanks argued that preparation — doing the work no one sees — is what separates professionals from amateurs. Whether you are an actor preparing a role, a doctor researching a diagnosis, or a student preparing a college application, the quality of your invisible work determines the quality of your visible results.

3. Hard Times Make You Who You Are

Hanks was candid about the struggles in his early life — a difficult childhood, years of financial instability, and the rejection that comes with any creative career. He argued that the Class of 2011 — graduating into the worst job market since the Great Depression — had already received a great gift: the experience of overcoming difficulty. That experience would make them better.

About Yale University

Yale University is one of the oldest and most prestigious universities in the United States, founded in 1701. Located in New Haven, Connecticut, Yale is consistently ranked among the world's top five universities. Its alumni include five U.S. Presidents, 62 living billionaires, and countless leaders in law, medicine, government, and the arts. Learn more in our Yale admissions guide for international students.

About the Speaker

Tom Hanks is one of the most decorated actors in Hollywood history, having won back-to-back Academy Awards for Best Actor for Philadelphia (1994) and Forrest Gump (1995). He attended Chabot College before transferring to California State University Sacramento, where he studied theater. He received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2016. His films, including Cast Away, Saving Private Ryan, and The Green Mile, have grossed billions worldwide.

See also: Hillary Clinton's 2018 Yale Class Day speech — another memorable address from a Yale Law alumna who went on to shape history.

posts.title: Hillary Rodham Clinton, 2018 Yale Class Day Speaker
posts.excerpt: Hillary Clinton returned to Yale in 2018 — wearing a Russian hat — and delivered a funny, fierce, and deeply serious speech about truth, democracy, and the stakes of public service for the next generation.
posts.content:
Hillary Clinton at Yale: Truth, Democracy, and a Russian Hat

In 2018, Hillary Rodham Clinton — Yale Law School Class of 1973 — returned to her alma mater to address Yale's graduating class. The speech was everything you'd expect from one of the most consequential politicians of the past three decades: sharp, funny, and beneath the jokes, deeply serious about the state of American democracy.

The Russian Hat Moment

Clinton arrived on stage wearing an elaborate Russian fur hat, a knowing reference to the investigations into Russian interference in the 2016 election. The audience erupted. It was a masterclass in deflecting criticism with self-deprecating humor — a political skill Clinton displayed throughout the address. For students studying communications or politics, this moment is worth studying as carefully as the words themselves.

Truth Is Not Negotiable

The speech's serious core was an impassioned defense of truth in public life. Clinton argued that the erosion of shared facts — the rise of disinformation, alternative facts, and media fragmentation — poses one of the greatest threats to democratic governance. She challenged Yale graduates, many of whom would enter law, medicine, finance, and public service, to be defenders of evidence and truth in their professional lives.

"A politics of hope — not fear. Of inclusion — not division. Of truth — not lies."

Public Service as a Moral Calling

Clinton's most moving passage was about why she has dedicated her life to public service despite its personal costs. She spoke about what drives people who choose careers in government, diplomacy, and advocacy — not wealth or fame, but the belief that their work makes a difference in people's lives. For students considering careers in public service, law, or policy, this is an important speech to watch in full.

About Yale Law School

Yale Law School is consistently ranked as the number one law school in the United States. Its graduates include Supreme Court Justices, U.S. Presidents, Senators, and leading academics across every area of law. Competition for admission is extraordinary — Yale Law accepts fewer than 10% of applicants. Clinton herself attended Yale Law School where she met Bill Clinton, who also graduated from Yale Law. Explore our Yale admissions guide and learn about the broader top law schools in the U.S.

About the Speaker

Hillary Rodham Clinton is a Yale Law School graduate, former First Lady of the United States, former U.S. Senator from New York, former Secretary of State under President Obama, and the first woman nominated for President by a major U.S. political party (2016). She has written numerous books including her memoir Living History and What Happened. She continues to be active in public life through the Clinton Foundation.

See also: Bill Clinton's profile — a Georgetown and Yale Law graduate who became the 42nd President of the United States.

posts.title: Elon Musk USC Commencement Speech 2014
posts.excerpt: Elon Musk's 2014 USC Marshall commencement speech is one of the most direct, practical, and unusual graduation addresses ever. His advice on hard work, useful knowledge, and "physics-based" thinking is genuinely different from anything else you'll hear.
posts.content:
Elon Musk at USC: Blunt, Practical, Uniquely Useful

In 2014, Elon Musk — CEO of Tesla and SpaceX, co-founder of PayPal — delivered the commencement address to USC Marshall School of Business graduates. Unlike most graduation speeches that lean on inspiration and platitudes, Musk's address was characteristically direct, almost blunt, and organized around genuinely practical advice. It has become one of the most rewatched commencement speeches in history.

Work Harder Than Anyone Else

Musk's first piece of advice was simply: work harder than you think is necessary. He spoke about working 100-hour weeks in the early days of Zip2, PayPal, Tesla, and SpaceX. He wasn't romanticizing overwork — he was being honest about what it takes to build things from scratch that have never existed before. For USC graduates entering competitive careers and industries, this was a sobering and energizing message.

Seek Critical Feedback

Musk's second point was about feedback. He argued that most people surround themselves with people who tell them what they want to hear. The path to improvement, he said, is actively seeking out people who will tell you what is wrong with your work. This requires a level of ego detachment that is genuinely rare — and genuinely valuable. In a college admissions context, this means getting real, critical feedback on your essays from people who will be honest with you, not just supportive.

Physics as a Framework for Thinking

Perhaps Musk's most distinctive advice was to use the mental frameworks of physics — first principles reasoning, working from the ground up rather than by analogy — to solve problems. He credited physics training with giving him the tools to question assumptions that others treat as fixed. This kind of reasoning is exactly what top universities try to cultivate, and what makes USC's engineering and business programs so valuable.

About USC Marshall

The USC Marshall School of Business is one of the top business schools in the United States, located in the heart of Los Angeles. USC Marshall alumni lead companies across entertainment, technology, finance, and healthcare. With strong ties to Hollywood and Silicon Valley alike, USC is one of the best schools in the country for students interested in business, entrepreneurship, or the intersection of technology and media. Explore our profiles of Marc Benioff (Salesforce CEO, USC Marshall) and Will Ferrell (USC) for examples of the range of careers USC produces.

About the Speaker

Elon Musk attended the University of Pennsylvania, where he earned degrees in economics and physics. He moved to Stanford for a PhD in energy physics before leaving after two days to co-found Zip2. He later co-founded PayPal, founded SpaceX, and became CEO of Tesla. He is currently also the owner of X (formerly Twitter) and the founder of xAI. His companies collectively employ over 100,000 people. See his full profile on our alumni page.

Related: Steve Jobs' 2005 Stanford commencement — another tech visionary who gave unusually practical graduation advice.

posts.title: Oprah Speaks to the Stanford Class of 2008
posts.excerpt: Oprah Winfrey's 2008 Stanford commencement address is a personal, powerful speech about following your calling, embracing failure, and using your platform to serve others.
posts.content:
Oprah at Stanford: A Speech About Feelings, Failure, and Finding Your Calling

In June 2008, Oprah Winfrey took the podium at Stanford University's graduation ceremony and delivered one of the most personal commencement addresses in recent memory. This was not a polished keynote with talking points — it was a deeply honest account of how she built a life guided by intuition, stumbled repeatedly, and ultimately found her calling in serving others.

The Speech's Central Insight: Your Feelings Are Data

Oprah opened with a striking idea: that feelings — not logic, not strategy — are our most reliable guide. She described learning to listen to the still, small voice within herself even when external signals pointed elsewhere. For Stanford graduates about to enter high-pressure careers in Silicon Valley and beyond, this was a counterintuitive but powerful message.

"Feelings are really your GPS system for life. When you're supposed to do something or not supposed to do something, your emotional guidance system lets you know."

Failure Is Not the Opposite of Success

Oprah spoke candidly about being fired from her first television job as a reporter, being told she was "unfit for television," and the years of struggle before The Oprah Winfrey Show became a cultural phenomenon. Her point was direct: failure is not the opposite of success, it is part of it. Every stumble contains the seed of something greater — if you're willing to dig for it.

Service as a Path to Significance

The speech's emotional climax was Oprah's distinction between success and significance. She challenged Stanford graduates — among the world's most talented and privileged — to move beyond personal success and toward service. "You will find true success," she said, "and happiness if you have only one goal — to fulfill the highest, most truthful expression of yourself as a human being."

What College Applicants Can Learn

For students applying to Stanford and other elite universities, Oprah's speech is a reminder that what admissions officers ultimately want to understand is: who are you, really? Not your test scores. Not your résumé. Your values, your resilience, and your vision for what you want to do in the world. The students who get into Stanford are not simply the most accomplished — they are the most authentic and purposeful.

About the Speaker

Oprah Winfrey grew up in poverty in rural Mississippi and overcame a childhood of severe hardship to become one of the most influential figures in American media history. She hosted The Oprah Winfrey Show for 25 seasons (1986–2011), launched OWN (Oprah Winfrey Network), and built a media empire estimated at $2.5 billion. The Oprah Winfrey Leadership Academy Foundation has provided education to thousands of girls in South Africa and beyond.

Also explore: Natalie Portman's Harvard journey — another powerful story of combining a high-profile career with serious academic ambition.

posts.title: Steve Jobs’ 2005 Stanford Commencement Address
posts.excerpt: Steve Jobs' legendary 2005 Stanford commencement speech — "Stay Hungry, Stay Foolish" — is considered one of the greatest graduation addresses of all time. Here's why every college applicant should watch it.
posts.content:
Why This Speech Matters for College Applicants

Delivered to Stanford's Class of 2005, Steve Jobs' commencement address is widely regarded as one of the greatest speeches ever given at a university graduation. Jobs — who dropped out of Reed College after just six months — told three stories from his life that have resonated with millions of students and entrepreneurs around the world.

The Three Stories

1. Connecting the Dots

Jobs described how dropping out of Reed College actually freed him to audit calligraphy classes — knowledge that directly shaped the beautiful typography of the original Macintosh computer ten years later. His lesson: you can only connect the dots looking backward. For college applicants, this is a powerful reminder that every experience, even unconventional ones, can become part your story. The courses you take, the clubs you join, and even your setbacks all become material that colleges want to read about in your essays.

2. Love and Loss

Jobs spoke candidly about being fired from Apple — the company he founded — and how that loss led him to start NeXT and Pixar, and ultimately return to Apple to build the iMac, iPod, and iPhone. His message: "You've got to find what you love." This resonates deeply for students choosing a college major or career path. The admissions process rewards students who have genuine passion, not résumé-padding.

3. Death

Perhaps the most famous section of the speech, Jobs described how confronting his mortality — including a cancer diagnosis — clarified what truly mattered. "Stay hungry. Stay foolish." These four words have become a battle cry for generations of entrepreneurs and thinkers who refuse to settle.

What College Applicants Can Learn

Jobs' speech is a masterclass in authentic storytelling — exactly what top colleges are looking for in your personal essays. Stanford, Harvard, and the other schools featured on Top College Planning don't just want to see your GPA and test scores. They want to understand who you are, what drives you, and how you think about failure and resilience.

Notice how Jobs structures his speech around three specific, personal stories — not abstract advice. This is precisely the technique the best college essays use. Don't write about what you think colleges want to hear. Write about what genuinely shaped you.

About the Speaker

Steve Jobs (1955–2011) co-founded Apple Inc. in 1976 with Steve Wozniak and Ronald Wayne. Despite dropping out of Reed College, he became one of the most transformative figures in the history of technology and design. Jobs gave this address to Stanford's graduating class just one year after being diagnosed with pancreatic cancer. He passed away in October 2011 at the age of 56.

Related reading: Explore our profile of Bill Gates, who also dropped out of an elite university to change the world, or read our guide on why Ivy League admission is so competitive — and what you can do about it.

posts.title: Bill Gates Harvard Commencement Address 2007
posts.excerpt: Bill Gates returned to Harvard in 2007 to deliver a commencement address — 32 years after dropping out. Here's what he said, why it matters, and what every college applicant can learn from the world's most famous dropout.
posts.content:
The Most Famous Harvard Dropout Returns

In 2007, Bill Gates stood before Harvard's graduating class — 32 years after dropping out of the same institution as a sophomore to co-found Microsoft. Harvard awarded him an honorary degree that day. His speech was self-deprecating, funny, and ultimately serious: a call to use the privilege of elite education to address the world's most pressing inequalities.

Key Themes from the Address

Complexity Is Not an Excuse for Inaction

Gates argued that the world's biggest problems — disease, poverty, climate change — are solvable with the tools and knowledge that elite university graduates possess. He challenged the Harvard class to ask: "How can the world's poorest people get the same breakthroughs in health, technology, and business that we take for granted?" For students applying to top universities, this is a reminder of the responsibility that comes with elite education.

Optimism as a Strategy

Gates is deeply optimistic — not naively, but strategically. He believes in using data, technology, and capital to make progress measurable. This is a mindset that top colleges actively seek in applicants. In your essays and interviews, demonstrate that you think about problems systematically and believe solutions are within reach.

The Privilege of Elite Education

Gates acknowledged that being at Harvard puts graduates in the top fraction of 1% of the world's most fortunate people. He challenged graduates to spend time — not just money — on the problems that affect those outside their bubble. This perspective is increasingly valued in college admissions, where community impact and global awareness are weighted heavily.

What College Applicants Can Learn

Gates famously left Harvard before completing his degree — and yet returned as one of its most celebrated alumni. This reminds students that the college you attend is a starting point, not an ending point. What you do with your education, your network, and your opportunities matters far more than the name on your diploma.

For students applying to Harvard or other Ivy League schools: colleges are not looking for students who will simply succeed individually. They are looking for students who will make the world better. Gates' speech is a perfect articulation of that expectation.

About the Speaker

Bill Gates co-founded Microsoft in 1975 after dropping out of Harvard University, where he had been studying pre-law. He served as Microsoft's CEO until 2000, building it into the world's largest software company. Through the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, he has committed the vast majority of his wealth to global health and education. Gates is one of the most prominent alumni featured in our alumni spotlight.

Also watch: Mark Zuckerberg's Harvard commencement address in 2017, delivered by another famous dropout who returned in triumph.

posts.title: President Obama at Michigan Commencement 2010
posts.excerpt: President Obama's 2010 University of Michigan commencement address is a passionate defense of democracy, civil discourse, and the obligation of educated citizens to engage — not retreat — from public life.
posts.content:
Obama at Michigan: A Defense of Democracy and Civil Discourse

On May 1, 2010, President Obama delivered the commencement address at the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor, speaking to one of the largest graduating classes in the school's history. His speech was not simply a graduation address — it was a pointed and urgent defense of democratic institutions at a time of growing political polarization in America.

The Core Argument: Democracy Needs Participants, Not Spectators

Obama's central message was that democracy only works when citizens engage actively with it — not just during elections, but every day. He challenged Michigan graduates to resist the temptation of cynicism, to engage with people they disagree with, and to do the hard work of building a more just society rather than watching from the sidelines.

"What's required is the same perseverance and determination that you've shown getting to this day. If you're willing to do your part, I promise you a better day is coming."

The Problem of Cynicism

Obama spoke specifically about how cynicism — the belief that nothing can change and that engagement is pointless — is one of the greatest threats to democratic society. This is a lesson that resonates far beyond politics. In your career, your academic work, and your community, cynicism is the enemy of progress. The students who succeed at top universities and in their careers are those who believe solutions are possible.

Civility in Disagreement

With remarkable foresight, Obama warned against the kind of political tribalism that would define the decade that followed. He challenged graduates to engage with opposing views seriously and charitably, to see those who disagree not as enemies but as fellow citizens with different perspectives. This kind of intellectual humility and open-mindedness is exactly what top universities cultivate — and what they look for in applicants.

About the University of Michigan

The University of Michigan is one of the world's great public universities, consistently ranked among the top 25 universities globally. Michigan alumni include Presidents, Nobel laureates, Supreme Court Justices, astronauts, and CEOs of many of America's largest companies. Read our full profile of the University of Michigan or our guide on how to apply to UMich.

About the Speaker

Barack Obama, a Columbia and Harvard Law graduate, served as the 44th President of the United States from 2009 to 2017. Before his presidency, he represented Illinois in the U.S. Senate. A constitutional law professor at the University of Chicago, Obama has delivered some of the most celebrated commencement speeches in modern history. See his full alumni profile here.

Also watch: Roger Federer's 2024 Dartmouth address — a similarly compelling speech about resilience, effort, and what it means to compete at the highest level.

posts.title: Conan O’Brien Delivers Dartmouth’s Commencement Address
posts.excerpt: Conan O'Brien's legendary 2011 Dartmouth commencement address is the funniest graduation speech ever given — and one of the most genuinely moving. His message about failure, reinvention, and joy is timeless.
posts.content:
Conan's Dartmouth Speech: Funnier Than a Late-Night Monologue, Deeper Than Most Sermons

Conan O'Brien delivered the 2011 Dartmouth College commencement address at one of the strangest moments in his career: shortly after losing The Tonight Show to Jay Leno in a very public, very painful transition that was followed by tens of millions of viewers. What Conan did with that experience — and what he told Dartmouth's graduating class about it — became one of the most memorable and frequently quoted commencement speeches of the 21st century.

The Most Honest Thing a Celebrity Has Said at a Graduation

Most commencement speakers at elite universities arrive triumphant — decorated with success, ready to dispense wisdom from the mountaintop. Conan arrived differently. He arrived having just been publicly humiliated, robbed of a job he had dreamed of for years, and uncertain about what came next. And he used that experience to tell graduates something genuinely true:

"It is our failure to become our perceived ideal that ultimately defines us and makes us unique. Your perceived failure can become a catalyst for profound reinvention."

Conan and Dartmouth

Conan O'Brien is a Harvard graduate — not a Dartmouth alumnus — which made his selection as Dartmouth's commencement speaker all the more interesting. (He joked about this at length, to great effect.) Dartmouth has a long history of selecting commencement speakers from the world's most interesting thinkers and entertainers, reflecting its commitment to intellectual life outside the classroom. Notable Dartmouth alumni include Dr. Seuss and Conan O'Brien — wait, scratch that last one. See our Dartmouth coverage for more on this fascinating Ivy League school.

The Core Message: Reinvention Is the Point

Conan's real message was that the version of success you have in mind right now is almost certainly wrong — and that's wonderful. The path from Harvard graduate to failed Tonight Show host to beloved late-night icon on TBS and now a wildly popular podcast was not planned. It happened through stumbling, adapting, and staying curious. For college applicants and new graduates, this is enormously liberating: you are not locked into the version of yourself you imagine today.

What College Applicants Can Learn

If you are applying to top universities and worrying about what happens if you don't get into your first-choice school, Conan's speech is essential medicine. The students who do the most interesting things in the world are often not the ones who got into their dream school on the first try. They are the ones who kept showing up, kept working, and stayed open to where the path led. Read our guide on why Ivy League admission is so difficult — and remember that what you do after matters more than which school accepted you.

About the Speaker

Conan O'Brien graduated magna cum laude from Harvard University in 1985 with a degree in history and literature. He was president of the Harvard Lampoon. He worked as a writer for Saturday Night Live and The Simpsons before taking over Late Night in 1993. His podcast Conan O'Brien Needs a Friend is consistently one of the most downloaded comedy podcasts in the world. See his alumni profile for more on his Harvard connection.

posts.title: Conan O’Brien Addresses The Harvard Class Of 2000
posts.excerpt: Conan O'Brien returned to Harvard in 2000 to address the graduating class — and delivered one of the funniest, most unexpected commencement addresses the Ivy League has ever seen.
posts.content:
Conan at Harvard: A Prodigal Son Returns

Long before his legendary 2011 Dartmouth address, Conan O'Brien returned to his alma mater Harvard University in 2000 to deliver the Class Day address to the graduating class. The speech set the tone for what would become Conan's signature commencement style: self-deprecating, absurdist, surprisingly moving, and entirely unlike anything else you will hear on a graduation stage.

Harvard's Most Famous Comedy Writer Reflects on the Ivy League

Conan graduated magna cum laude from Harvard in 1985 and was president of the Harvard Lampoon — the storied humor magazine that has launched the careers of countless comedy writers, directors, and performers. When he returned to Harvard in 2000, he was already a late-night legend, having hosted NBC's Late Night with Conan O'Brien for seven years.

His address to the Harvard Class of 2000 was a meditation on what Harvard actually prepares you for — and what it doesn't. He was characteristically honest: Harvard teaches you to think, to argue, to analyze. But it does not teach you how to handle failure, rejection, or the vast uncertainties of a creative career. Those lessons, he said, come from life.

The Harvard Network — and Its Limits

One of the most interesting threads in Conan's Harvard speech is his complicated relationship with the Harvard network. He acknowledged that Harvard connections opened doors throughout his career — but that once inside those rooms, what mattered was the quality of your work, not your diploma. This is worth remembering for students who fixate on getting into Harvard as an end in itself. Harvard is a door-opener. What you do on the other side of the door is entirely up to you.

Comedy as a Lens on Truth

Conan argues, implicitly throughout his Harvard speech, that comedy is one of the most honest forms of human communication. A good joke requires truth — it requires seeing reality as it actually is, not as we wish it to be, and finding the incongruity between the two. This is not so different from what the best college essays do: they see something clearly and communicate it honestly, without pretense.

About Harvard University

Harvard University, founded in 1636, is the oldest university in the United States and consistently ranks as the most prestigious in the world. With an endowment of over $50 billion and alumni including eight U.S. Presidents, 161 Nobel laureates, and leaders in virtually every field, Harvard remains the most sought-after undergraduate institution on the planet. Read our complete guide to applying to Harvard as an international student for a detailed breakdown of the admissions process.

Notable Harvard alumni featured on this site include Bill Gates, Mark Zuckerberg, Natalie Portman, and Conan O'Brien himself.

About the Speaker

Conan O'Brien (Harvard Class of 1985) is one of the most successful comedy writers and performers of his generation. After Harvard, he wrote for Saturday Night Live and The Simpsons before hosting Late Night with Conan O'Brien (1993–2009), The Tonight Show (2009–2010), and Conan on TBS (2010–2021). His podcast Conan O'Brien Needs a Friend has hundreds of millions of downloads and is consistently one of the most popular comedy podcasts in the world.

posts.title: Executive Education
posts.excerpt: Columbia Business School Executive Education: Elevate Your Career Columbia Business School Executive Education offers a range of programs designed to transform your professional life. Whether you’re an individual looking to enhance your skills or an organization seeking custom solutions, Columbia provides a comprehensive learning experience. Programs for Individuals Columbia’s programs immerse you in relevant information […]
posts.content:
Columbia Business School Executive Education: Elevate Your Career

Columbia Business School Executive Education offers a range of programs designed to transform seasoned professionals into sharper, more effective leaders. Whether you're a mid-career manager, a C-suite executive, or a founder preparing for scale, Columbia's programs deliver Ivy League-caliber instruction in formats designed for working professionals.

Program Formats

Columbia Business School Executive Education programs are offered in several formats to fit demanding schedules:

  • In-person programs on Columbia's Morningside Heights campus in New York City
  • Online and hybrid programs delivered through Columbia's digital learning platform
  • Custom programs designed for organizations seeking to upskill entire leadership teams

Programs range from two-day intensive workshops to multi-week certificate programs. Most do not require an MBA — they are designed for practitioners who bring real-world experience to the classroom.

Popular Program Areas

Columbia's executive education catalog spans a broad range of disciplines, including:

  • Finance and investment management
  • Marketing and brand strategy
  • Negotiation and leadership communication
  • Entrepreneurship and innovation
  • Technology and digital transformation
  • Healthcare management
  • Sustainability and ESG strategy

Many programs are led by the same faculty who teach in Columbia's full-time MBA program — giving participants access to world-class instruction alongside a cohort of peers from diverse industries and geographies.

The Value of Columbia's New York Location

Few universities in the world match Columbia's proximity to the industries it teaches. Located in Manhattan, Columbia Business School draws on relationships with Wall Street, global media companies, technology startups, and non-profit organizations to bring real-world context into every classroom. Executive education participants often describe the peer network — other senior professionals from finance, consulting, healthcare, and tech — as among the most valuable aspects of the experience.

Who Should Consider Executive Education?

Executive education is ideal for professionals who want to deepen specific skills, earn a credential without committing to a full degree program, or pivot into a new area of business. It is particularly valuable for professionals at the 5–15 year mark of their careers who are preparing for more senior roles or board-level responsibilities.

For students currently planning their undergraduate education, understanding that schools like Columbia offer lifelong learning pathways — not just four-year degrees — can be a compelling reason to invest in an Ivy League education. Alumni networks from schools like Columbia often provide privileged access to executive education programs, alumni pricing, and peer communities that extend well beyond graduation.

Learn more at the Columbia Business School Executive Education website, or explore our full Columbia University profile.

posts.title: Is there an exchange program within the Ivy League?
posts.excerpt: IvyPlus Exchange Scholar Program The IvyPlus Exchange Scholar Program enables a graduate student enrolled in a doctoral program in one of the participating institutions to study at one of the other participating graduate schools for a limited period of time at no additional tuition cost, in order to take advantage of educational opportunities not available on the student’s […]
posts.content:
Is There an Exchange Program Within the Ivy League?

Yes — graduate students at Ivy League and peer institutions can access a formal cross-enrollment program that allows them to take courses at member schools beyond their home university. Here's everything you need to know about the IvyPlus Exchange Scholar Program and similar opportunities across elite universities.

The IvyPlus Exchange Scholar Program

The IvyPlus Exchange Scholar Program enables doctoral students enrolled at one of the participating institutions to spend a semester or year studying at another member school. The program is open to PhD candidates who want access to faculty, libraries, or courses not available at their home institution.

Participating institutions in the IvyPlus consortium include:

  • Brown University
  • Columbia University
  • Cornell University
  • Dartmouth College
  • Harvard University
  • University of Pennsylvania
  • Princeton University
  • Yale University
  • Duke University
  • Johns Hopkins University
  • MIT
  • University of Chicago

Exchange scholars typically pay tuition at their home institution and receive library access, research facilities, and course enrollment privileges at the host school. This makes the program particularly valuable for doctoral candidates doing cross-disciplinary work or seeking mentorship from a specific faculty member at another institution.

What About Undergraduate Exchange?

Ivy League schools generally do not offer a formal cross-enrollment program for undergraduates in the same way that the IvyPlus program serves graduate students. However, some opportunities do exist:

  • Yale-Harvard Game cross-campus spirit: While the rivalry is intense, both schools allow limited course sharing for students with demonstrated academic need.
  • 12-College Exchange (New England Colleges): Several liberal arts colleges in New England participate in a consortium allowing students to spend a semester at a member school — though this doesn't include the Ivies themselves.
  • Visiting Student programs: Some Ivy League schools allow non-enrolled students to take courses as visiting or non-degree students, often through continuing education offices.
  • Study abroad: Many Ivy students spend a semester abroad through programs coordinated by their home institution, including partnerships with Oxford, Cambridge, and other global universities.

Five College Consortium and Similar Models

Outside the Ivy League, some university consortia offer more robust undergraduate exchange programs. The Five Colleges Consortium (Amherst, Hampshire, Mount Holyoke, Smith, and UMass Amherst) allows students at any member institution to take courses at the others for free. The Claremont Colleges operate similarly in Southern California. These models show what structured undergraduate exchange can look like when institutions choose to cooperate closely.

Making the Most of Your Resources

Even without a formal exchange, Ivy League students have access to an extraordinary range of academic resources within their home institution. Harvard, Yale, and Princeton each have multiple graduate schools, research centers, and interdisciplinary programs that give undergraduates access to resources far beyond a single college. Many students also pursue joint degrees, dual-degree programs, or summer research fellowships at peer institutions to broaden their academic horizons.

For more on choosing the right Ivy League school, read our FAQ on the college admissions process or explore our full directory of school profiles.

posts.title: UChicago vs Northwestern
posts.excerpt: UChicago vs. Northwestern — two world-class universities just 12 miles apart in the Chicago area. Which one is right for you? A detailed comparison of academics, culture, admissions, and career outcomes.
posts.content:
Two World-Class Universities, One Chicagoland Decision

The University of Chicago and Northwestern University are separated by just 12 miles along the shores of Lake Michigan — but they are worlds apart in culture, pedagogy, and the type of student they attract. If you are a high-achieving student trying to decide between these two elite institutions, this comparison will help you understand what each school is actually like and which is the better fit for you.

Academic Approach

University of Chicago

UChicago is famous — or notorious, depending on your perspective — for the rigor and intensity of its Core Curriculum, a two-year sequence of required courses in mathematics, natural sciences, humanities, and social sciences that every undergraduate must complete regardless of major. UChicago's academic culture rewards students who love ideas for their own sake, who enjoy debate and argumentation, and who don't mind challenging orthodoxies in every field. The unofficial motto — "The University of Chicago: Where Fun Comes to Die" — is a self-deprecating joke, but it contains a kernel of truth. Students here work very hard.

Northwestern

Northwestern offers a more flexible curriculum and a broader range of preprofessional programs, including its highly ranked Medill School of Journalism, Kellogg School of Management (undergraduate), Bienen School of Music, and one of the best theater programs in the country. Northwestern students can double-major across the arts and sciences more easily than at UChicago, and the campus culture is generally described as collaborative rather than competitive. Northwestern also competes in the Big Ten, giving it a more traditional college athletics experience.

Admissions Comparison

Both schools are extremely selective. UChicago's acceptance rate is approximately 5%, making it one of the five most selective universities in the United States. Northwestern's acceptance rate hovers around 7%. Both schools require the Common App, strong test scores (though both are currently test-flexible), and compelling essays. UChicago is famous for its quirky supplemental essay prompts, which reward students who are genuinely intellectually curious rather than formulaically accomplished.

Campus Life and Culture

UChicago's Hyde Park campus is beautiful but somewhat isolated — the school has worked hard in recent years to improve its relationship with surrounding neighborhoods and to build a more vibrant student life. Northwestern's Evanston campus sits directly on Lake Michigan and offers immediate access to both Evanston's restaurants and shops and Chicago's world-class cultural institutions just 30 minutes away by train.

Career Outcomes

Both schools produce graduates who go on to exceptional careers. UChicago is particularly dominant in finance, economics, and academia — its economics department has produced more Nobel laureates than any institution in the world (over 30). Northwestern excels in journalism, entertainment, consulting, and law. Both schools have strong alumni networks in Chicago and nationally.

Which Should You Choose?

Choose UChicago if you love ideas for their own sake, enjoy intellectual debate, are drawn to economics or hard science, and thrive in academically intense environments. Choose Northwestern if you want more flexibility, are interested in journalism, theater, music, or business, prefer a more traditional campus culture with athletics, or want easier access to a major city. Both are exceptional — the right choice depends entirely on who you are.

Explore more in our guide to why elite college admissions are so competitive, or read our in-depth profiles of Harvard and Stanford for comparison.

posts.content:
Careers in College Admissions Consulting

The college admissions industry has grown significantly over the past decade, creating a range of meaningful career opportunities for educators, counselors, former admissions officers, and subject-matter experts who want to guide the next generation of students.

Who Works in College Counseling?

College admissions consulting attracts professionals from a wide variety of backgrounds. Former admissions officers from schools like Harvard, Yale, and Stanford bring firsthand knowledge of how applications are reviewed. Teachers and school counselors often transition into independent advising after developing expertise over years in the classroom. Writers and editors with strong communication skills are sought after to help students craft compelling personal essays.

There are also opportunities in data analysis, curriculum design, test preparation, financial aid advising, and international student outreach — roles that are increasingly in demand as the admissions landscape grows more competitive and globally connected.

Skills That Transfer Well

If you have experience in higher education, secondary school counseling, academic coaching, or writing, you likely already have transferable skills. The most effective college counselors combine:

  • Deep knowledge of how selective admissions works — including holistic review, demonstrated interest, and the role of testing policies
  • Strong interpersonal skills to build trust with anxious students and parents
  • Clear writing and editing ability to coach students through the personal statement and supplemental essays
  • Organizational rigor to manage multiple students with overlapping deadlines

Career Paths in the Field

Common career paths include independent college consultant, school-based college counselor (public or private K–12), director of college counseling at a boarding or day school, or admissions strategist at a national tutoring company. Some professionals build their own boutique practices working with a small number of students per year; others join established firms serving hundreds of families annually.

Certification through the Higher Education Consultants Association (HECA) or the Independent Educational Consultants Association (IECA) can add credibility and open networking opportunities.

Current Openings

Top College Planning periodically partners with schools, tutoring centers, and admissions consulting firms to share job opportunities with our community. Check back here for listings, or reach out directly at careers@topcollegeplanning.com to express interest in future roles.

We are especially interested in hearing from former admissions officers, school counselors, and experienced college essay coaches. Whether you are looking for a part-time advisory role or a full-time position, we would love to connect.

posts.excerpt: What Is an MBA? An MBA (Master of Business Administration) is a prestigious graduate degree that equips professionals with essential management skills. Here’s what you need to know: Why People Quit Their Jobs for an MBA Executive MBA (EMBA) What Is an EMBA? An EMBA (Executive MBA) is similar to an MBA but tailored for current executives. Here’s how […]
posts.content:
What Is an MBA?

An MBA (Master of Business Administration) is a prestigious graduate degree that equips professionals with essential management skills. Here’s what you need to know:

  1. Curriculum: MBA programs cover a wide range of business areas, including accounting, marketing, finance, and project management. They emphasize analysis, strategy, and leadership development.
  2. Career Boost: MBAs are ideal for professionals aiming to advance their careers. Employers highly value MBA graduates, and job prospects are promising.
  3. Specializations: You can specialize in areas like finance, marketing, healthcare administration, and more, tailoring your MBA to your interests.
  4. Networking: MBA programs provide opportunities to build valuable relationships with fellow professionals.
  5. Cost Considerations: While tuition can be substantial, many organizations subsidize or sponsor employees’ MBAs.

Why People Quit Their Jobs for an MBA

  1. Career Advancement: Professionals seek MBAs to reach executive levels, enhance their skills, and unlock higher earning potential.
  2. Network Expansion: MBA programs facilitate networking, connecting students with influential peers and industry leaders.
  3. Industry Specialization: Specialized MBA tracks allow professionals to deepen expertise in specific fields.
  4. Personal Growth: Pursuing an MBA is a life-changing experience, enabling long-term career shifts and personal development.

Executive MBA (EMBA)

What Is an EMBA?

An EMBA (Executive MBA) is similar to an MBA but tailored for current executives. Here’s how it differs:

  1. Target Audience: EMBA students are mid- to senior-level managers with significant work experience (average age 32-38). They don’t need to quit their jobs to attend classes.
  2. Content Focus: EMBA programs delve into business nuances, leadership, and strategic thinking. They assume prior business knowledge.
  3. Flexible Scheduling: EMBA courses accommodate busy professionals, often held on weekends or weeknights.
  4. Credential: Both MBA and EMBA lead to the same Master of Business Administration degree.

In summary, whether you choose an MBA or EMBA depends on your career stage, goals, and lifestyle. Both paths offer valuable learning experiences and networking opportunities.

posts.title: Northwestern Tops SLP Program in the US
posts.excerpt: Northwestern University’s Speech-Language Pathology Program Ranks Top 5 in the United States In summary, Northwestern University’s SLP program equips graduates to make a significant impact in the field, nationally and internationally.
posts.content:
Northwestern University's Speech-Language Pathology Program Ranks Among the Best in the US

Northwestern University's Department of Communication Sciences and Disorders has long been recognized as one of the most prestigious programs in speech-language pathology (SLP) in the country. For students interested in pursuing a career helping individuals with communication and swallowing disorders, Northwestern offers a rigorous, research-driven path to professional excellence.

Why Northwestern's SLP Program Stands Out

Northwestern's program consistently ranks in the top five nationally by U.S. News & World Report. Located on the Evanston campus, the program benefits from its proximity to Chicago's extensive hospital network, offering students access to world-class clinical training at facilities like Northwestern Memorial Hospital and the Ann & Robert H. Lurie Children's Hospital of Chicago.

The curriculum covers the full spectrum of communication disorders, including:

  • Articulation and phonological disorders
  • Language delays and developmental disorders in children
  • Acquired neurological communication disorders (aphasia, dysarthria)
  • Fluency disorders including stuttering
  • Voice disorders and resonance
  • Dysphagia (swallowing disorders)
  • Autism spectrum disorder communication support

Graduate Admissions at a Glance

Admission to Northwestern's MS in Communication Sciences and Disorders is highly selective. Applicants typically hold a bachelor's degree with coursework in communication sciences, linguistics, or a related field. Strong GRE scores (where required), letters of recommendation, and a compelling personal statement are essential components of a competitive application.

Most admitted students hold GPAs of 3.5 or above. Research or clinical experience prior to application is strongly encouraged — students who have shadowed licensed speech-language pathologists or participated in undergraduate research tend to be more competitive applicants.

Career Outcomes

Graduates of Northwestern's SLP program pursue careers across a wide range of settings, including:

  • Medical settings: hospitals, rehabilitation centers, and skilled nursing facilities
  • Schools: working with children on language, literacy, and articulation
  • Private practice: operating independent clinics for targeted populations
  • Research: continuing to doctoral programs at institutions like Northwestern's own PhD program

The Bureau of Labor Statistics projects continued strong demand for speech-language pathologists, with employment expected to grow faster than the national average through the early 2030s.

Is Northwestern Right for You?

If you're drawn to a clinical career that blends neuroscience, education, and interpersonal care, Northwestern's SLP program is worth serious consideration. The combination of elite academic training, Chicago-area clinical opportunities, and a powerful alumni network makes it one of the top destinations for aspiring speech-language pathologists in the US.

For more information, visit the Northwestern Department of Communication Sciences and Disorders website. And if you're also weighing Northwestern's broader undergraduate offerings, read our UChicago vs Northwestern comparison to see how these two elite Chicago-area schools stack up.

posts.title: What is Ivy Day?
posts.excerpt: Ivy Day at the University of Pennsylvania is a long-standing tradition that celebrates the achievements of graduating seniors. Here’s a brief overview: For more detailed information, you can visit the official pages of the Ivy Day events and traditions. As part of the modern college admissions process, the term “Ivy Day” also refers to the day […]
posts.content:
What Is Ivy Day? Everything You Need to Know About Ivy League Decision Day

For college applicants to the eight Ivy League universities, the first week of April brings one of the most anticipated — and nerve-wracking — moments of the year: Ivy Day. On this single day, all eight Ivy League schools simultaneously release their Regular Decision admissions results, creating a nationally shared moment of anticipation, celebration, and disappointment.

When Is Ivy Day?

Ivy Day traditionally falls in the first week of April, usually on a Thursday or Friday. All eight Ivy League schools — Harvard, Yale, Princeton, Columbia, Penn, Brown, Dartmouth, and Cornell — release their decisions at the same time, which in recent years has been 5:00 PM ET. This coordinated release is unique to the Ivy League and has made Ivy Day a cultural moment that trends on social media every spring.

Which Schools Release on Ivy Day?

The eight schools that participate in the coordinated Ivy Day release are:

  • Harvard University
  • Yale University
  • Princeton University
  • Columbia University
  • University of Pennsylvania
  • Brown University
  • Dartmouth College
  • Cornell University

Note that other highly selective schools like MIT, Stanford, Duke, and the University of Chicago release their decisions around the same period but on their own separate schedules — they are not part of Ivy Day.

How to Check Your Decision

Decisions are released through each school's applicant portal, not by email. Log in to your application portal for each school you applied to starting at 5:00 PM ET on Ivy Day. Some portals load slowly due to heavy traffic — be patient and keep refreshing. Avoid relying on social media speculation; wait until you see your official status directly from the portal.

What Happens If You're Deferred or Waitlisted?

Many applicants receive neither an acceptance nor a rejection — they're waitlisted. Being waitlisted means the school is still considering you if space becomes available after the May 1 national deposit deadline. If you're waitlisted at a school you're still interested in, send a Letter of Continued Interest (LOCI) expressing your enthusiasm and any new accomplishments since you applied. See our guide on writing a strong LOCI for tips.

Preparing for Ivy Day Emotionally

Whatever the outcome, Ivy Day is a high-stakes emotional experience. Admissions at the most selective schools involves a significant element of chance — each year, thousands of academically exceptional students are turned away not because they weren't qualified, but because there simply isn't room. If you receive a rejection, know that it is not a judgment of your worth or your future. Many celebrated leaders, entrepreneurs, and innovators were rejected from Ivy League schools and went on to thrive at other universities.

The deadline to commit to your school of choice is typically May 1 — National Decision Day. Use the weeks between Ivy Day and May 1 to visit campuses, revisit financial aid packages, and make a thoughtful decision about where you'll spend the next four years.

For more on the admissions process, explore our FAQ page and our in-depth school profiles.

posts.title: Penn School Song Red and Blue
posts.excerpt: “The Red and Blue” is a popular song of Penn. According to pennathletics.com School Color There are several stories concerning the origin of Penn’s colors. One tale explains that George Washington, having been invited to a Pennsylvania Commencement to receive the first presidential honorary degree, donned his best uniform — blue tunic trimmed in red. Mention […]
posts.content:
"The Red and Blue" is one of the most beloved and recognized fight songs in the Ivy League. Associated with the University of Pennsylvania, the song has been a fixture of Penn athletic events, reunions, and ceremonies for well over a century — a musical symbol of Penn pride passed down through generations of Quakers.

History of "The Red and Blue"

"The Red and Blue" was written by Joseph Vance and has been sung at Penn since the late 19th century, making it one of the oldest collegiate fight songs in American history. The song celebrates Penn's iconic colors — the crimson red and the deep blue — which have represented the university since its founding.

The song is traditionally sung at Penn football games at Franklin Field, one of the oldest collegiate stadiums in the country, as well as at basketball games, reunions, and commencement ceremonies. It is a rallying cry that connects current students to more than 250 years of Penn tradition.

Penn's School Colors: Red and Blue

Penn's official colors are red and blue — specifically, Penn Red (a deep crimson) and Penn Blue (a rich, dark navy). These colors have been associated with the University of Pennsylvania since the 19th century and appear across athletic uniforms, official merchandise, and campus signage.

Penn's teams compete as the Quakers, a nickname rooted in Philadelphia's rich Quaker heritage. The combination of the Quakers identity and the red-and-blue color scheme gives Penn one of the most distinctive and historically grounded athletic brands in college sports.

Penn Athletics and the Ivy League

Penn competes in the Ivy League across 33 varsity sports. Like all Ivy League schools, Penn does not offer athletic scholarships — all student-athletes are admitted through the same academic process as the general student body, with coaches providing recommendations to the admissions office. Penn has produced numerous Olympic athletes and professional players across sports including basketball, rowing, lacrosse, and track and field.

Franklin Field, Penn's home stadium, hosted the first Army-Navy game and has been home to the Penn Relays — one of the oldest and largest track and field competitions in the world — since 1895. It's a venue that connects Penn's athletic tradition directly to American sports history.

Life at Penn: More Than Academics

School spirit at Penn is expressed through much more than a song. Penn students embrace the broader culture of the campus: the competitive intellectual energy of Wharton, the innovation of Penn Engineering, the creative community at the College of Arts and Sciences, and the social energy of Locust Walk. Whether you're singing "The Red and Blue" at a football game or crossing the stage at commencement in Spruce Hill, being a Quaker is a lifelong identity.

Explore our full University of Pennsylvania school profile to learn more about Penn's admissions statistics, financial aid, and campus life. And check our Notable Alumni page to see the remarkable Quakers who have gone on to shape the world.

posts.title: M7 Business School: Chicago Booth
posts.excerpt: The M7 business schools form an elite group of seven prestigious institutions renowned for their top-tier MBA programs.
