alumni.bio: Shonda Rhimes is the most powerful showrunner in television history. An MFA graduate of USC's School of Cinematic Arts, she created Grey's Anatomy, Scandal, and How to Get Away with Murder — shows that dominated Thursday nights on ABC for years. In 2017 she signed a landmark deal with Netflix, and her production company Shondaland continues to define prestige television.
alumni.degree: MFA Screenwriting, USC School of Cinematic Arts

alumni.title: Peter Thiel
alumni.excerpt: PayPal co-founder, Facebook's first outside investor, and author of Zero to One.
alumni.bio: Peter Thiel co-founded PayPal with Elon Musk, became Facebook's first outside investor with a $500K check that returned over $1B, and founded Palantir Technologies. A Stanford philosophy and law graduate, he is one of Silicon Valley's most influential and controversial thinkers. His book Zero to One is considered essential reading for entrepreneurs worldwide.

alumni.title: Reed Hastings
alumni.excerpt: Netflix co-founder who turned DVD rentals into a global streaming empire with 260M subscribers.
alumni.bio: Reed Hastings co-founded Netflix in 1997 and built it into the world's leading streaming service with over 260 million subscribers across 190 countries. A Stanford computer science graduate and former Peace Corps volunteer, Hastings revolutionized how the world consumes entertainment — toppling Blockbuster and forcing Hollywood to reinvent itself.

alumni.title: Phil Knight
alumni.excerpt: Nike co-founder who built the world's most iconic sportswear brand from a handshake deal.
alumni.bio: Phil Knight co-founded Nike in 1964 out of the trunk of his car after meeting legendary track coach Bill Bowerman at the University of Oregon. After earning his MBA at Stanford, Knight built Nike into the world's most valuable sports brand — worth over $30 billion — through iconic athlete partnerships with Michael Jordan, Tiger Woods, and countless others. His memoir Shoe Dog is one of the best business books ever written.
alumni.degree: MBA, Stanford Graduate School of Business

alumni.title: Condoleezza Rice
alumni.excerpt: 66th U.S. Secretary of State — the first Black woman to hold the office.
alumni.bio: Condoleezza Rice made history as the 66th U.S. Secretary of State — the first Black woman to hold the office — serving under President George W. Bush from 2005 to 2009. She earned her PhD in political science from Stanford, where she also served as Provost, and was National Security Advisor during the September 11 attacks. A concert-level classical pianist, she remains a professor at Stanford's Hoover Institution.

alumni.title: Marcus Allen
alumni.excerpt: USC Heisman winner and Raiders legend — one of the greatest running backs in NFL history.
alumni.bio: Marcus Allen won the Heisman Trophy at USC in 1981 and went on to become one of the most versatile running backs in NFL history. He won a Super Bowl with the Los Angeles Raiders and was later a key piece of the Kansas City Chiefs' revival under coach Marty Schottenheimer. He is a Pro Football Hall of Famer and one of the most celebrated athletes in USC Trojan history.

alumni.title: Reggie Bush
alumni.excerpt: USC electrifying tailback, Super Bowl champion, and one of college football's most exciting players ever.
alumni.bio: Reggie Bush was the most electrifying player in college football during his time at USC, winning the Heisman Trophy in 2005 — which was later vacated due to NCAA violations and then officially restored in 2021. He was the second overall pick in the 2006 NFL Draft by the New Orleans Saints, with whom he won Super Bowl XLIV. Known for his explosive speed, agility, and highlight-reel plays, Bush is one of the most exciting players in USC and NFL history.

alumni.title: Kareem Abdul-Jabbar
alumni.excerpt: UCLA three-time NCAA champion and NBA's all-time leading scorer — one of the greatest players ever.
alumni.bio: Born Lew Alcindor, Kareem Abdul-Jabbar dominated college basketball at UCLA from 1966 to 1969, winning three consecutive NCAA national championships and never losing a collegiate game in the Final Four. He went on to become the NBA's all-time leading scorer with 38,387 points across a 20-year career with the Milwaukee Bucks and Los Angeles Lakers, winning 6 NBA championships. He is widely regarded as one of the two or three greatest basketball players in history.

alumni.title: Jackie Robinson
alumni.excerpt: UCLA multi-sport star who broke MLB's color barrier — one of the most courageous figures in American history.
alumni.bio: Before breaking Major League Baseball's color barrier with the Brooklyn Dodgers in 1947, Jackie Robinson was one of the most remarkable multi-sport athletes in UCLA history, lettering in baseball, basketball, football, and track and field — the first athlete to do so at the school. He left UCLA before graduating to support his family, then served in WWII before his historic MLB debut. His courage on and off the field helped transform American society and he is one of the most important figures in 20th century history.

alumni.title: Bill Walton
alumni.excerpt: UCLA's two-time national champion — one of the greatest college basketball players and NBA champions ever.
alumni.bio: Bill Walton was the centerpiece of John Wooden's legendary UCLA dynasty, winning two consecutive NCAA national championships (1972, 1973) and finishing his college career as arguably the greatest college center ever. He went on to win NBA championships with the Portland Trail Blazers and Boston Celtics, and was inducted into the Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame. After his playing career, Walton became a beloved and colorful basketball broadcaster. He passed away in 2024.

alumni.title: Eileen Gu
alumni.excerpt: Olympic triple medalist, global supermodel, and Stanford student — one of the most extraordinary young athletes alive.
alumni.bio: Eileen Gu (顾艾琳) is one of the most remarkable young athletes of her generation. Born in San Francisco to a Chinese mother, she chose to compete for China at the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics and made history by winning 2 gold medals (halfpipe and big air) and 1 silver medal (slopestyle) — the most decorated freestyle skier in a single Winter Olympics. A straight-A student, she was admitted to Stanford University, where she enrolled in 2023, studying while managing a global modeling career that has made her one of the most recognized athletes in the world.

alumni.title: Kwong Man Wai
alumni.excerpt: Hong Kong fencing gold medalist and Stanford student — a trailblazer for Asian athletes on the global stage.
alumni.bio: Kwong Man Wai is a Hong Kong fencing gold medalist who has combined elite athletic achievement with academic excellence at Stanford University. As part of Hong Kong's rising fencing program, Kwong represents a new generation of Asian athletes who excel at the highest international level while pursuing world-class education. His story resonates deeply with international students from Asia who aspire to balance competitive sports and elite university study at schools like Stanford.

alumni.title: Shoban Haghey
alumni.excerpt: University of Michigan student-athlete and inspiring figure for international students pursuing excellence in the U.S.
alumni.bio: Shoban Haghey is a University of Michigan student-athlete whose journey represents the ambition and determination of international students who pursue both academic and athletic excellence in the United States. Michigan's Big Ten athletic tradition and world-class academics make it a compelling destination for talented international students, and Haghey's story is an inspiring example of what is possible when passion meets opportunity at one of America's greatest universities.

alumni.title: Bill Nye
alumni.content:
Bill Nye: Cornell's Science Guy

Bill Nye — widely known as "The Science Guy" — earned his B.S. in Mechanical Engineering from Cornell University in 1977, where he studied under the legendary astronomer Carl Sagan. That mentorship left a lasting impression, sparking Nye's lifelong passion for making science accessible to everyday audiences.

After Cornell, Nye worked as an engineer at Boeing in Seattle before pivoting to comedy and science communication. His television series Bill Nye the Science Guy, which ran from 1993 to 1998, won 19 Emmy Awards and became a staple in classrooms across North America. The show made complex scientific concepts — from evolution to climate change to Newton's laws — entertaining and digestible for children and adults alike.

Nye has remained connected to Cornell throughout his career. In 2011, he was elected Chairman of the Board of the Planetary Society, the world's largest space-interest organization, and has received honorary degrees and recognition from universities worldwide. He returned to Cornell in 2011 to deliver the Meinig School of Biomedical Engineering Commencement Address.

Today, Bill Nye continues to advocate for science education and climate action, demonstrating how a rigorous engineering education from Cornell can launch a career far beyond the lab or factory floor.
alumni.bio: Beloved TV science educator and mechanical engineer, Bill Nye studied under Carl Sagan at Cornell before winning 19 Emmy Awards for making science fun and accessible to millions of children worldwide.

alumni.title: Ruth Bader Ginsburg
alumni.content:
Ruth Bader Ginsburg: Cornell's Legal Pioneer

Before becoming one of the most celebrated Supreme Court Justices in American history, Ruth Bader Ginsburg graduated from Cornell University in 1954 with a B.A. in Government, finishing first in her class. At Cornell, she studied constitutional law under Professor Robert Cushman, who inspired her commitment to civil liberties, and met her future husband, Martin Ginsburg, who would become her lifelong partner and champion.

After Cornell, Ginsburg attended Harvard Law School — one of only nine women in a class of more than 500 — before transferring to Columbia Law School, where she graduated tied for first in her class. She went on to become the second woman ever confirmed to the U.S. Supreme Court, nominated by President Bill Clinton in 1993, and served until her death in 2020.

Known affectionately as "The Notorious RBG," Ginsburg authored landmark opinions on gender equality and voting rights, and became a cultural icon whose influence extended well beyond the courtroom. She championed equal rights for women at a time when discrimination was legally sanctioned and argued six landmark gender-discrimination cases before the Supreme Court before ever joining it.

Cornell's campus honors her legacy through the Ruth Bader Ginsburg '54 Society, which supports women at the university, and the law library collection she helped build. Her Cornell years gave her both the intellectual foundation and personal relationships that shaped her extraordinary life of service.
alumni.bio: Pioneering Supreme Court Justice and champion of gender equality, Ruth Bader Ginsburg graduated first in her class from Cornell's Government program before becoming "The Notorious RBG" and a global icon of justice.

alumni.title: Toni Morrison
alumni.content:
Toni Morrison: Cornell's Nobel Laureate

Toni Morrison — one of the greatest American novelists of the 20th century — earned her M.A. in English from Cornell University in 1955, writing her thesis on the theme of suicide in the works of William Faulkner and Virginia Woolf. It was at Cornell that she deepened the literary sensibility that would later produce some of the most powerful novels in American literature.

Morrison went on to write eleven novels, including Beloved, Song of Solomon, The Bluest Eye, and Sula. Her work explored the African American experience with extraordinary depth, poetic language, and moral urgency. Beloved, based on the true story of an enslaved woman, won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1988 and the American Book Award.

In 1993, Morrison was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature — the first Black American woman to receive the honor. The Swedish Academy praised her for novels that "give life to an essential aspect of American reality" with "visionary force and poetic import."

After teaching at Texas Southern University, Howard University, and Yale, Morrison joined the faculty at Princeton University, where she taught creative writing until her retirement in 2006. She passed away in 2019, but her legacy endures in every classroom where her books are taught and every reader who encounters her voice for the first time.
alumni.bio: Nobel Prize-winning author of Beloved and Song of Solomon, Toni Morrison earned her M.A. from Cornell before becoming the first Black American woman to win the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993.

alumni.title: Vera Wang
alumni.content:
Vera Wang: Cornell's Fashion Icon

Global fashion designer Vera Wang graduated from Cornell University in 1971 with a B.A. in Art History, an education that gave her the visual and cultural literacy that would underpin one of the most recognizable names in high fashion. At Cornell, Wang also pursued her passion for figure skating, training seriously enough to compete for a spot on the 1968 U.S. Olympic team — a goal she narrowly missed.

After Cornell, Wang spent 15 years at Vogue magazine, rising to become one of its most senior fashion editors before moving to Ralph Lauren as a design director. In 1990, she founded Vera Wang Bridal House in New York City, driven by her frustration at finding elegant, modern bridal gowns for her own wedding. The boutique quickly became the destination for brides seeking sophisticated design.

Today, the Vera Wang brand encompasses bridal wear, ready-to-wear fashion, accessories, home goods, and fragrances, with flagship stores across the United States and Asia. Her gowns have been worn by Michelle Obama, Ivanka Trump, Kim Kardashian, Jennifer Lopez, and virtually every major celebrity bride of the last three decades.

Wang has been recognized with numerous industry awards, including the Geoffrey Beene Lifetime Achievement Award from the CFDA. Her path from Cornell art history student to globally recognized couturier is a testament to how a broad liberal arts education can fuel creative entrepreneurship at the highest level.
alumni.bio: World-renowned bridal and fashion designer Vera Wang studied Art History at Cornell and competed in figure skating before building a global fashion empire dressing celebrity brides for over three decades.

alumni.title: Ratan Tata
alumni.content:
Ratan Tata: Cornell's Global Business Leader

One of the world's most respected industrialists, Ratan Tata graduated from Cornell University's College of Architecture in 1962 with a B.S. in Architecture. His time at Cornell shaped not only his aesthetic sensibility but also his appreciation for systems thinking — a quality that would define his approach to building one of India's largest and most diversified conglomerates.

After Cornell, Tata joined the family business, Tata Group, working on the shop floor of Tata Steel before rising through the ranks. He became Chairman of Tata Sons in 1991 and spent more than two decades transforming the group from a predominantly India-focused industrial company into a global powerhouse. Under his leadership, Tata completed landmark acquisitions including Tetley Tea (2000), Corus Steel (2007), and most famously Jaguar Land Rover from Ford Motor Company in 2008.

Tata is also celebrated for the Tata Nano — a revolutionary attempt to produce the world's most affordable car to make personal transportation accessible to hundreds of millions of Indians. While commercially mixed, the project embodied his commitment to democratizing opportunity.

Beyond business, Tata is renowned for his philanthropy. Two-thirds of the equity of Tata Sons is held by charitable trusts, funding education, healthcare, and rural development across India. Cornell University honored his impact by naming the Tata-Cornell Institute for Agriculture and Nutrition in his honor. He passed away in October 2024, leaving behind a legacy of ethical, purpose-driven leadership admired the world over.
alumni.bio: Chairman of the Tata Group and one of India's most beloved industrialists, Ratan Tata studied architecture at Cornell before leading Tata Group to global acquisitions including Jaguar Land Rover and building one of the world's most admired philanthropic empires.
